perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means all information relating to the Project o r the Contractor’s proposal for the Project and all information relating to the nature of either Party’s business, affairs or trade secrets, which either Party directly or indirectly receives or acquires from the other Party, or the other Party’s represent ative, either in writing or verbally (but in the case of verbal information, only that which is reduced to writing by the Disclosing Party and delivered to the Receiving Party within 10 days of the verbal disclosure) , or through observation of the Project, except information falling into any one or more of the following categories: (a) information which the Receiving Party can show was in its possession on a non - confidential basis before receipt of the information from the Disclosing Party; (b) information which is lawfully in the public domain at the time of the Receiving Party’s receipt of the information from the Disclosing Party, but not including the Company’s request for proposals for the Project; (c) information which, after the Receiving Party’s receipt of the i nformation from the Disclosing Party, becomes part of the public domain through no act of the Receiving Party or of any third party under an obligation of confidence with respect to such information, but only after such information becomes part of the publ ic domain; or (d) information which, after receipt of the information by the Receiving Party, is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a third party, but only after such information is so received, and provided such third party is under no obligation o f confidence with respect to such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means all information relating to the Project or the Contractor’s proposal for the Project and all information relating to the nature of either Party’s business, affairs, or trade secrets, which either Party directly or indirectly receives or acquires from the other Party, or the other Party’s representative, either in writing or verbally, except information falling into any one or more of the following categories: (a) information which the Receiving Party can show was in its possession before receipt of the information from the Disclosing Party; (b) information which is in the public domain at the time of the Receiving Party’s receipt of the information from the Disclosing Party; (c) information which, after the Receiving Party’s receipt of the information from the Disclosing Party, becomes part of the public domain through no act of the Receiving Party; or (d) information which is obtained by the Receiving Party from a third party.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of confidential information includes specific conditions and limitations. The modified version removes these conditions, broadening the scope of what is excluded from confidential information. Specifically, the requirement for verbal information to be reduced to writing within 10 days for it to be considered confidential is removed. Also, the modified text removes the phrase 'lawfully' from options (b) and (d) which changes the legal definition of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes key protections afforded to trade secrets under UTSA. By removing the requirement for verbal information to be reduced to writing and diluting requirements for 'lawfully' obtained data, it contradicts the due diligence expected in protecting trade secrets, potentially jeopardizing their legal status.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language included specific conditions for information to be considered confidential, such as the requirement for verbal disclosures to be reduced to writing within 10 days. The changed text removes these conditions, broadening the exceptions to what is considered confidential information. The perturbation explanation indicates that this change weakens the protection of confidential information, and the Law Explanation suggests that it undermines due diligence expected in protecting trade secrets. Although the scraped snippets are not helpful, the removal of conditions directly modifies the original obligation, therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Any disclosure of any Confidential Information to the Receiving Party made during the Initial Term shall continue to be subject to this Agreement for a period of 2 years thereafter, provided that any trade secret shall be entitled to protection for the life of the trade secret.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Any disclosure of any Confidential Information to the Receiving Party made during the Initial Term shall continue to be subject to this Agreement for a period of 6 months thereafter, provided that any trade secret shall only be entitled to protection for those 6 months.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause protects trade secrets for their entire lifespan, as is legally standard. The modified clause limits this protection to just 6 months which would render the trade secret unprotected after such time, contradicting standard trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the protection of trade secrets to 6 months contradicts this principle, as the law generally intends for trade secrets to be protected indefinitely, so long as secrecy is maintained. After the 6 months, any information may be disclosed

perturbation.justification: The changed text limits trade secret protection to 6 months, contradicting the UTSA's principle of indefinite protection as long as secrecy and competitive advantage are maintained, as indicated in the Law Explanation. The original text protected it for its lifespan. The Uniform Law Commission snippet does not provide details of laws, but it reinforces that laws are designed to provide clarity, which is undermined by the change.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Except as expressly permitted by this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not disclose the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and shall use reasonable care, which is at least the same degree of care that the Receiving Party ordinarily uses with respect to its own proprietary information, in relation to the use, transmittal and storage of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Except as expressly permitted by this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not disclose the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and shall use minimal care, which is far below the degree of care that the Receiving Party ordinarily uses with respect to its own proprietary information, in relation to the use, transmittal and storage of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires 'reasonable care' which is standard for NDAs. The change to 'minimal care' which is 'far below' the Receiving Party's own proprietary information standards dilutes this obligation, providing inadequate protection for the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information and contradicting standard reasonable care requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (First) of Torts § 757 states that one who discloses or uses another's trade secret, without a privilege to do so, is liable to the other if (a) he discovered the secret by improper means, or (b) his disclosure or use constitutes a breach of confidence reposed in him by the other in disclosing the secret to him. By switching 'reasonable care' to 'minimal care', the receiving party can effectively act with significantly less care and disclose the information without consequence, contradicting the expected standard of care under tort law for protecting confidential information and trade secrets. The law would typically find someone liable if they did not act with a reasonable standard of care.

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'reasonable care' with 'minimal care,' which is explicitly stated to be 'far below' the standard the Receiving Party uses for its own information. The Law Explanation says this contradicts the expected standard of care under tort law for protecting confidential information. The original text aligns with a reasonable standard, while the changed text creates a situation where the receiving party can act with significantly less care without consequence, directly undermining the protection afforded to the disclosing party's information. While the provided law snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation are strong enough to conclude that the change creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8.8 This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted under the laws of the Province of Alberta and the Parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Province of Alberta for all disputes arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.8 This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted under the laws of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea and the Parties consent to the jurisdiction of the courts of the Province of Alberta for all disputes arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change specifies that the laws of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea (North Korea) govern the agreement, while maintaining the jurisdiction of Alberta courts. This creates a conflict because North Korean law may not be recognized or enforceable in Alberta courts, leading to significant uncertainty about which legal principles will be applied.
perturbation.law_explanation: The doctrine of comity dictates that courts of one jurisdiction will defer to the laws and judicial decisions of another jurisdiction out of deference and mutual respect. However, this deference is generally not extended to jurisdictions with legal systems that are fundamentally incompatible with the forum jurisdiction's principles of law and justice. Selecting North Korean law as governing law directly clashes with the principle of comity because the Province of Alberta, and more broadly the legal system of Canada, will not typically defer to the laws of North Korea due to fundamental differences in legal systems, human rights records, and principles of fairness and due process. This creates uncertainty in enforcement, as an Alberta court may refuse to apply North Korean law, rendering the choice of law clause unenforceable and causing the court to apply its own law instead.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Hilton v. Guyot | 159 U.S. 113 (1895) | Justia U.S. Supreme Court Center Hilton v. Guyot, 159 U.S. 113 (1895) Overview Opinions Syllabus U.S. Supreme Court Hilton v. Guyot, 159 U.S. 113 (1895) Hilton v. Guyot Nos. 130, 34 Argued April 10, 1894 Decided June 3, 1895 159 U.S. 113 Syllabus A citizen and resident of this country who has his principal place of business here but has an agent in a foreign country and is accustomed to purchase and store large quantities of goods there, and, in a suit brought against him by a citizen and in a court of that country, appears and defends with the sole object of preventing his property within the jurisdiction, but not in the custody of that court, from being taken in satisfaction of any judgment that may be recovered against him there cannot, in an action brought against him in this country upon such a judgment, impeach it for want of jurisdiction of his person. The admission at the trial in a court of a foreign country, according to its law and practice, of testimony not under oath and without opportunity of cross-examination, and of documents with which the defendant had no connection and which by our law would not be admissible against him, is not of itself a sufficient ground for impeaching the judgment of that court in an action brought upon it in this country. When an action is brought in a court of this country by a citizen of a foreign country against one of our own citizens to recover a sum of money adjudged by a court of that country to be due from the defendant to the plaintiff, and the foreign judgment appears to have been rendered by a competent court, having jurisdiction of the cause and of the parties, and upon due allegations and proofs and opportunity to defend against them, and its proceedings are according to the course of a civilized jurisprudence, and are stated in a clear and formal record, the judgment is prima facie evidence, at least, of the truth of the matter adjudged, and the judgment is conclusive upon the merits tried in the foreign co
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction. The original text specifies that the contract is governed by the laws of Alberta. The changed text replaces this with the laws of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea (North Korea) while maintaining the jurisdiction of Alberta courts. The Law Explanation states that comity, which dictates courts of one jurisdiction will defer to the laws and judicial decisions of another jurisdiction, is generally not extended to jurisdictions with legal systems that are fundamentally incompatible with the forum jurisdiction's principles of law and justice. The Alberta court might refuse to apply North Korean law. This conflict arises because North Korean law may not be recognized or enforceable in Alberta courts, leading to uncertainty about which legal principles will be applied. The provided scraped snippets do not directly address this issue, but the Law Explanation, combined with the severe differences in legal systems, indicates a significant legal contradiction.
